subject: Physics
q: The ratio of maximum acceleration to maximum velocity in a simple harmonic motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> At, <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> the displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum acceleration? The initial phase is <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> .
question: The ratio of maximum acceleration to maximum velocity in a simple harmonic motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> At, <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> the displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum acceleration? The initial phase is <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> .
solution:
In a simple harmonic motion,

Maximum acceleration, fmax=ω2A

Maximum velocity, vmax=Aω. 

fmaxvmax=ω2AAω=ω

ω=10

Displacement in SHM is x=Asin⁡ωt+π4

At t=0

5=Asin⁡π4

A=52. 

Max acceleration = ω2A

=100×52

=5002  m s-2

Comparing the given equation y=Asinπt+ϕ with a standard equation y=Asinωt+ϕ. We have

ω=π rad s-1

Since time period is  T=2πω=2 s, it is a second's pendulum.

T=2πLg⇒L=gπ2=99.4 cm

subject: Physics
q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion (SHM). The ratio of potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle when its displacement is half of its amplitude will be
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion (SHM). The ratio of potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle when its displacement is half of its amplitude will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the potential energy of the particle executing SHM is given by

P=12mω2x2   ...1

The formula to calculate the kinetic energy of the same particle is given by

K=12mω2A2-x2   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

PK=12mω2x212mω2A2-x2=x2A2-x2   ...3

Substitute A2 for x into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

PK=A22A2-A22= 13

subject: Physics
q: If a spring has time period T, and is cut into n equal parts, then the time period of each part will be
question: If a spring has time period T, and is cut into n equal parts, then the time period of each part will be
solution:
Spring constant becomes $n$ times for each piece. $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{k}}$
$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2}=\frac{\sqrt{n K}}{K} \text { or } T_2=T / \sqrt{n}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The point A moves with a uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius 0.36 m and covers 30° in 0.1 s. The perpendicular projection P from A on the diameter MN represents the simple harmonic motion of P. The restoration force per unit mass when P touches M will be :
question: The point A moves with a uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius 0.36 m and covers 30° in 0.1 s. The perpendicular projection P from A on the diameter MN represents the simple harmonic motion of P. The restoration force per unit mass when P touches M will be :
solution:
The point A covers 30° in 0.1 s.
Then, we have π6 rad⟶0.1 s.
Or 1 rad⟶0.1π6 s
Or 2π in→ 0.1×6π×2π
Thus, time taken is T=1.2 sec.

We know that ω=2πT
Then, ω=2π1.2
Restoration force (F)=mω2A.

Then Restoration force per unit mass Fm=ω2 A
Fm=2π1.22×0.36

≅9.87 N

If pressed downward slightly and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion at angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of the bottle is <math><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is close to: <math><mo>(</mo></math> density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>)</mo></math>
question: A cylindrical plastic bottle of negligible mass is filled with <math><mn>310</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>m</mi><mi>l</mi></math> of water and left floating in a pond with still water. If pressed downward slightly and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion at angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of the bottle is <math><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is close to: <math><mo>(</mo></math> density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Let at equilibrium, y length of bottle is immerged in water:

So, mg=fbuo

mg=ρAyg ..... (1) [Equilibrium Condition]

Now it is further pushed by distance x

Frestoring=ρ[Ax+y)g-mg

mω2x=ρAxg+ρAyg-mg

ω=ρAgm=ρAgρV=πr2gV=7.9 rad/sec

subject: Physics
q: When a particle executes simple Harmonic motion, the nature of graph of velocity as function of displacement will be
question: When a particle executes simple Harmonic motion, the nature of graph of velocity as function of displacement will be
solution:
The displacement equation of a particle is given by 

x=Asinωt+ϕ⇒sinωt+ϕ=xA

The velocity equation is given by,

v=ωAcosωt+ϕ⇒cosωt+ϕ=vωA

Since, sin2θ+cos2θ=1

⇒x2A2+v2ω2A2=1

The above equation represents an ellipse.

The frequency of the oscillation is:
solution:
a=Asin⁡ωt0...(1)
b=Asin⁡2ωt0...(2)
c=Asin⁡3ωt0...(3)

adding equation (1) and (3)

a+c=Asin⁡ωt0+sin⁡3ωt0=2Asin⁡2ωt0cos⁡ωt0...(4)

∵  sinC+sinD=  2sinC+D2cosC−D2

from equation (2) and (4)

a+cb=2cosωt0

ω= 1t0cos-1⁡a+c2b ⇒f=12πt0cos-1⁡a+c2b

subject: Physics
q: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. Its speed is increased to three times at an instant when its displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The new amplitude of motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____.
question: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. Its speed is increased to three times at an instant when its displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The new amplitude of motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Speed in SHM in terms of displacement is given by, v=ωA2-x2.

at x=2A3, we get

v=ωA2-2A32=5Aω3

Let the new amplitude be=A'

Then, 

v'=3v⇒ωA'2-2A32=5Aω

⇒A'=73A

Therefore, n=7.

subject: Physics
q: The equation of motion of a particle is given by $x = a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ cm. The particle will come to rest at time $t_1$ and it will have zero acceleration at time $t_2$. The $t_1$ and $t_2$ respectively are _______.
question: The equation of motion of a particle is given by $x = a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ cm. The particle will come to rest at time $t_1$ and it will have zero acceleration at time $t_2$. The $t_1$ and $t_2$ respectively are _______.
solution:
The equation of motion is given by $x = a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$.

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement:
$v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = 50a \cos\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$

The particle comes to rest when $v = 0$. For the first time it comes to rest:
$\cos\left(50t_1 + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = 0$

$50t_1 + \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

$50t_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$

$t_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{300} \text{ s}$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$A = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = -2500a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$

The particle has zero acceleration when $A = 0$. For the first time it has zero acceleration:
$\sin\left(50t_2 + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = 0$

$50t_2 + \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \pi$

$50t_2 = \pi - \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

$t_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{150} = \dfrac{\pi}{75} \text{ s}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\pi}{300} \, s, \dfrac{\pi}{75} \, s$

q: If a simple harmonic motion is represented by $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}+\alpha \mathrm{x}=0$, its time period is
question: If a simple harmonic motion is represented by $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}+\alpha \mathrm{x}=0$, its time period is
solution:
$\omega^2=\alpha$
$\omega=\sqrt{\alpha}$
$T=\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{\alpha}}$

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies A and B of equal mass are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively. If the bodies oscillate vertically such that their amplitudes are equal, the ratio of the maximum velocity of $A$ to the maximum velocity of $B$ is
question: Two bodies A and B of equal mass are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively. If the bodies oscillate vertically such that their amplitudes are equal, the ratio of the maximum velocity of $A$ to the maximum velocity of $B$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_1=\mathrm{A}_1 \omega_1 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_2=\mathrm{A}_2 \omega_2 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~m}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~K}_2}{\mathrm{~m}}}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~K}_2}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The time period of a simple harmonic oscillator is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$. Measured value of mass $(m)$ of the object is 10 g with an accuracy of 10 mg and time for 50 oscillations of the spring is found to be 60 s using a watch of 2 s resolution. Percentage error in determination of spring constant $(k)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ \%.
question: The time period of a simple harmonic oscillator is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$. Measured value of mass $(m)$ of the object is 10 g with an accuracy of 10 mg and time for 50 oscillations of the spring is found to be 60 s using a watch of 2 s resolution. Percentage error in determination of spring constant $(k)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ \%.
solution:
The given formula for time period is
$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$.
Squaring both sides, we get $T^2 = 4\pi^2 \frac{k}{m}$, which gives $k = \frac{4\pi^2 m}{T^2}$.
The relative error in $k$ is given by $\frac{\Delta k}{k} = \frac{\Delta m}{m} + 2\frac{\Delta T}{T}$.
Given: $m = 10$ g, $\Delta m = 10$ mg $= 0.01$ g.
Total time for $n=50$ oscillations is $t = 60$ s with resolution $\Delta t = 2$ s.
Since $T = t/n$, the relative error in $T$ is the same as in $t$: $\frac{\Delta T}{T} = \frac{\Delta t}{t}$.
Substituting the values: $\frac{\Delta k}{k} = \frac{0.01}{10} + 2\left(\frac{2}{60}\right)$.
$\frac{\Delta k}{k} = 0.001 + \frac{2}{30} = 0.001 + 0.0666...$
Percentage error $= \left(0.001 + \frac{1}{15}\right) \times 100 = 0.1 + \frac{100}{15} = 0.1 + 6.666... = 6.766...\%$.
Rounding to two decimal places, we get $6.76\%$.

subject: Physics
q:
A light hollow cube of side length 10 cm and mass 10 g , is floating in water. It is pushed down and released to execute simple harmonic oscillations. The time period of oscillations is $y \pi \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}$, where the value of $y$ is
(Acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, density of water $=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ )
question:
A light hollow cube of side length 10 cm and mass 10 g , is floating in water. It is pushed down and released to execute simple harmonic oscillations. The time period of oscillations is $y \pi \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}$, where the value of $y$ is
(Acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, density of water $=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ )
solution:
Additional buoyant force
$\begin{aligned} & g \rho a^2 x=\sigma a^3 A \\ & A=\frac{\rho}{\sigma} \frac{g}{a} x \\ & T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma a}{\rho g}} \end{aligned}$ Now, $\sigma=\frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}=10$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 0.1}{10^3 \times 10}} \\ & =2 \pi \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

q: Two particles $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of equal masses are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$, respectively. If the maximum velocities, during oscillation, are equal, the ratio of amplitude of $A$ and $B$ is
question: Two particles $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of equal masses are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$, respectively. If the maximum velocities, during oscillation, are equal, the ratio of amplitude of $A$ and $B$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\max }=\omega \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{k}$
$\Rightarrow \omega^2=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}} \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}}} \quad \Rightarrow \omega \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{K}} \quad$ or $\quad \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_2}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}} \max =\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}} \max \quad \Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{~m}}}\right)\left(\mathrm{A}_1\right)=\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{~m}}}\right)\left(\mathrm{A}_2\right) \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{A}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}}$

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus square of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> ) graph for a simple pendulum executing simple harmonic motion.
question: Choose the correct length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus square of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> ) graph for a simple pendulum executing simple harmonic motion.
solution:
A simple pendulum consists of a point mass attached to an inextensible string and suspended from a support.

The formula to calculate the time period T of a simple pendulum is given by

T=2πlg.......................(1)

where, l is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the relation between the time period and the length.

T2=4π2g×𝓁T2∝l............................(2)

Equation (2) suggests that the graph between the square of the time period versus the length of the pendulum is a straight line passing through the origin.

subject: Physics
q: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, attached to a horizontal spring, executes SHM with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When this mass passes through its mean position, then a smaller mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>124</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is placed over it and both masses move together with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, attached to a horizontal spring, executes SHM with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When this mass passes through its mean position, then a smaller mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>124</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is placed over it and both masses move together with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
At mean position, the velocity of the particle in S.H.M. is maximum, so kinetic energy is KE=12kA2, where, k is spring constant.

We also know that, kinetic energy is related to momentum as KE=p22m.

Now, from both equations, we have 

12kA2=p22m     (Here, momentum is conserved)

⇒A1 A22=m2 m1=1024900

⇒A1 A2=3230=1615=1616-1

∴α=16

subject: Physics
q: A mass $\mathrm{M}$ is suspended from a spring of negligible mass. The spring is pulled a little and then released so that the mass executes SHM of time period T. If the mass is increased by $\mathrm{m}$, the time period becomes $\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}}{3}$. Then the ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}$ is
question: A mass $\mathrm{M}$ is suspended from a spring of negligible mass. The spring is pulled a little and then released so that the mass executes SHM of time period T. If the mass is increased by $\mathrm{m}$, the time period becomes $\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}}{3}$. Then the ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{K}}}$
$\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}}{3}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}}}$
Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i), $\frac{5}{3}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}}$. Squaring both the sides $\frac{25}{9}=\frac{M+m}{M}=1+\frac{m}{M} \Rightarrow \frac{m}{M}=\frac{25}{9}-1=\frac{16}{9}$

subject: Physics
q: When the displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is one third of its amplitude, the ratio of total energy to the kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.
question: When the displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is one third of its amplitude, the ratio of total energy to the kinetic energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.
solution:
The total energy of the harmonic oscillator is given by the formula

E=12kA2   ...1

where, k is the force constant and A is the amplitude.

The potential energy of the oscillator at the given displacement can be written as

U=12kA32=kA22×9=E9

Hence, its kinetic energy is given by

KE=E-E9=8E9

Thus, the required ratio is

EKE=E8E9=98

Hence, x=9.

subject: Physics
q: A ring is hung on a nail. It can oscillate, without slipping or sliding (i) in its plane with a time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and (ii) back and forth in a direction perpendicular to its plane, with a period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
question: A ring is hung on a nail. It can oscillate, without slipping or sliding (i) in its plane with a time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and (ii) back and forth in a direction perpendicular to its plane, with a period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
solution:
The time period of oscillation of physical pendulum is given by

T=2πIMgl  ...(1)

Where, I is the moment of inertia of ring about given axis and l is the distance of centre of mass of ring from the axis of rotation.

Moment of inertia of ring in its plane is given by

I1=Ic+ Md2=MR2+MR2=2MR2

Moment of inertia of ring perpendicular to its plane is given by

I2=Ic+ Md2=MR22+MR2=32MR2

Now the ratio of time period is given by 

T1T2=I1I2

⇒T1T2=2MR232MR2

⇒T1T2=43=23

subject: Physics
q: Bob of a simple pendulum of length $l$ is made of iron. The pendulum is oscillating over a horizontal coil carrying direct current. If the time period of the pendulum is $\mathrm{T}$ then :
question: Bob of a simple pendulum of length $l$ is made of iron. The pendulum is oscillating over a horizontal coil carrying direct current. If the time period of the pendulum is $\mathrm{T}$ then :
options: $T < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is smaller than in air alone.
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone.
$T>2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is smaller than in air alone.
$T < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone.
solution:
When the pendulum is oscillating over a current carrying coil, and when the direction of oscillating pendulum bob is opposite to the direction of current. Its instantaneous acceleration increases.
Hence time period $\mathrm{T} < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{g}}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone due energy dissipation.

subject: Physics
q:
In a linear Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

(A) Restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement.

(B) The acceleration and displacement are opposite in direction.

(C) The velocity is maximum at mean position.

(D) The acceleration is minimum at extreme points.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In a linear Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

(A) Restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement.

(B) The acceleration and displacement are opposite in direction.

(C) The velocity is maximum at mean position.

(D) The acceleration is minimum at extreme points.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The equations of SHM is given by

F=–kx

⇒a=–ω2x

Velocity is given by, v=ωA2-x2.

Clearly, velocity is maximum at mean position and acceleration is maximum at extreme positions.

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
q: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion are found to have magnitudes of $4 \mathrm{~m}, 2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $16 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ at a certain instant. The amplitude of the motion is $\sqrt{x}, \mathrm{~m}$ where $x$ is ________
question: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion are found to have magnitudes of $4 \mathrm{~m}, 2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $16 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ at a certain instant. The amplitude of the motion is $\sqrt{x}, \mathrm{~m}$ where $x$ is ________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & x=4 \mathrm{~m}, V=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{a}=16 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & |\mathrm{a}|=\omega^2 \mathrm{x} \\ & \Rightarrow 16=\omega^2(4) \\ & \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\omega \sqrt{\mathrm{A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2} \\ & A=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\omega^2}+\mathrm{x}^2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{4}+16} \\ & A=\sqrt{17} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$, is expressed as $x(t)=A \sin \omega t$, where $A$ is the amplitude. The maximum value of potential energy of this oscillator is found at $t=T / 2 \beta$. The value of $\beta$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The displacement of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$, is expressed as $x(t)=A \sin \omega t$, where $A$ is the amplitude. The maximum value of potential energy of this oscillator is found at $t=T / 2 \beta$. The value of $\beta$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For SHM with $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, the potential energy is
$U = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2 = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$.
Maximum potential energy occurs when $\sin^2(\omega t) = 1$, i.e., when $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, ...$
The first maximum occurs at $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{2}$, giving $t = \frac{\pi}{2\omega} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{T}{2\pi} = \frac{T}{4}$.
Since this is given as $t = \frac{T}{2\beta}$.
We have $\frac{T}{4} = \frac{T}{2\beta}$, so $\beta = 2$.
